items.myth: Zero correlation always means the variables are independent.
items.reality: Independence implies zero covariance (hence zero Pearson correlation when defined), but the converse fails in general. Only under special assumptions—such as joint normality—does uncorrelated imply independent.
items.example: Let X be uniform on [-1, 1] and Y = X². Then Cov(X, Y) = 0 but Y is completely determined by X.

items.myth: High correlation proves one variable causes the other.
items.reality: Correlation measures statistical association, not causal direction. Confounders, reverse causation, and selection can produce strong r without a direct causal link.
items.example: Ice-cream sales and drowning deaths correlate in summer because temperature affects both—not because ice cream causes drowning.

items.myth: The correlation matrix and covariance matrix are interchangeable without consequence.
items.reality: They encode the same linear structure up to scaling, but algorithms using Σ (PCA on unstandardized data, Gaussian density) depend on actual variances; correlation standardizes variances to 1.
items.example: PCA on covariance emphasizes high-variance features; PCA on correlation treats all features equally on the diagonal.

items.myth: Cov(X, Y) and Cov(Y, X) can differ.
items.reality: Covariance is symmetric: Cov(X, Y) = Cov(Y, X). The covariance matrix is symmetric with identical off-diagonal entries.
items.example: Swapping columns in `np.cov(x, y)` still yields the same upper-right off-diagonal entry.
